channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Andy Weir on the Science That Inspired His Novel ‘Project Hail Mary’
item.description: Andy Weir discusses his science-fueled novel “Project Hail Mary,” which has been adapted into a film that opens in theaters on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Jonathan Olley/Amazon MGM Studios, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Andy Weir, author of the sci-fi book “Project Hail Mary,” on the set of the movie adaptation, which he produced and which opens Friday.

item.title: Gerd Faltings of Germany Wins 2026 Abel Prize in Mathematics
item.description: Gerd Faltings proved a conjecture that had been unsolved for six decades, using connections between numbers and geometry.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.category: Norwegian Academy of Science and Letters

item.title: NASA’s Hubble Telescope Spots Comet K1 Exploding Into Fragments
item.description: In a stroke of luck, astronomers saw the comet C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) break into four or five fragments in November after it passed close to the sun.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:credit: NASA, ESA, Dennis Bodewits (AU); Image Processing: Joseph DePasquale (STScI)

item.title: Why Some Birds Seem to Be Developing a Cigarette Habit
item.description: Research from Poland adds to evidence from Latin American countries that compounds in used cigarette butts ward off parasites in bird nests.
item.dc:creator: Lesley Evans Ogden
item.media:credit: Gillian Pullinger/Alamy
item.media:description: A blue tit nesting in a cigarette disposal bin in Surrey, England.

item.title: How To Bring a Bird’s Song Back from the Edge of Extinction
item.description: In a new study, wild regent honeyeaters became vocal tutors, teaching their disappearing song to birds in a captive breeding program.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: RCAustralia/Alamy
item.media:description: The regent honeyeater population of Australia has dropped sharply since the mid-20th century. Today scientists estimate that roughly 250 wild birds live in the fragmented forests across an area about the size of Spain.

item.title: Spaceflight Started 100 Years Ago in a Massachusetts Cabbage Patch
item.description: Before humanity sent satellites, telescopes, humans and weapons into space, Robert Goddard experimented with the first liquid-fueled rocket on his aunt’s farm.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan O’Callaghan
item.media:credit: Bettmann/Getty Images
item.media:description: Dr. Goddard at Clark University in Worcester, Mass., in the 1920s.

item.title: NASA Says Artemis II Moon Launch Is On Track for April 1
item.description: After postponing launch opportunities in February and March, the agency determined that four astronauts could proceed toward the first crewed lunar journey in more than 50 years.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: John Raoux/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Artemis II rocket returned from the launchpad to the Vehicle Assembly Building at the Kennedy Space Center after the mission was scrubbed in February.

item.title: A Meteor Streaks Across the U.S. and Rattles Ohio With an Explosive Boom
item.description: NASA confirmed that the loud boom heard and felt by people in northern Ohio was indeed caused by a meteor.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser

item.title: Space Jam: NASA’s MADCAP Team Directs Traffic at the Moon
item.description: A “red alert” involving the private Blue Ghost mission in lunar orbit a year ago highlights a growing number of incidents above Earth’s neighbor.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Firefly Aerospace, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A view from the Blue Ghost lander as it orbited the moon at an altitude of about 62 miles on Feb. 24, 2025.

item.title: 24 States Sue E.P.A. Over Climate Change Decision
item.description: The suit accuses the agency of illegally repealing the endangerment finding, the scientific assessment that required it to regulate greenhouse gases.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Brian Snyder/Reuters
item.media:description: Andrea Joy Campbell, the attorney general of Massachusetts, which is leading the lawsuit along with California, New York and Connecticut.

item.title: The Weather Is Getting Wilder, and Some See a Dire Signal in the Data
item.description: Several of the Earth’s systems are changing faster than predicted as global temperatures rise, scientists say.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research
item.media:credit: Christinne Muschi/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A car crushed under a downed tree after strong winds in Montreal, Canada, on Tuesday.

item.title: Ozempic Is About to Go Generic in India, China and Canada
item.description: In India, China and several other nations, Novo Nordisk is on the verge of losing patent protection for its blockbuster weight loss drug, opening the door for cheaper competing versions.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins, Meaghan Tobin, Eshe Nelson, Alex Travelli and Pragati K.B.
item.media:credit: Saumya Khandelwal for The New York Times

item.title: FEMA Will Relaunch Climate Resiliency Grants
item.description: A judge ruled in December that the agency could not cancel a program that had helped states invest billions of dollars in disaster readiness.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Cliff Owen/Associated Press
item.media:description: Noel Boxer, an external affairs officer with FEMA, inspects the flow of raw sewage near the Potomac River in Cabin John, Md., on March 14, 2026.

item.title: James Barnard, a Global Force in Wastewater Treatment, Dies at 90
item.description: An environmental engineer, he invented a biological method to remove nitrogen and phosphorous from wastewater, an advance that transformed the industry worldwide.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: As a Meningitis Outbreak Spreads in Kent, UK, Here’s What to Know
item.description: At least 27 young adults are believed to be infected, and two have died, in an outbreak of meningococcal disease in the county of Kent, in southeast England.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine
item.media:credit: Carl Court/Getty Images
item.media:description: Students and faculty members wait in line for antibiotics at the University of Kent in Canterbury, England, on Monday.

item.title: Women Who Undergo Menopause Before 40 Face Higher Heart Attack Risk
item.description: A new study found that women who went through so-called premature menopause had 40 percent more fatal and nonfatal heart attacks over the course of their lives.
item.dc:creator: Roni Caryn Rabin
item.media:credit: Chris Gallagher/Science Source
item.media:description: An echocardiogram of a female patient. The average age of menopause, which is the end of menstruation and is defined as 12 months without a period, is 51 to 52.

item.title: Democrats Hammer Trump on ‘Energy Affordability’
item.description: A new Senate report argues the Trump administration has also pushed costs up by stifling clean energy.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer of New York, the minority leader, right, released the report with Senator Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island, left, the top Democrat on the environment committee, along with Senator Martin Heinrich of New Mexico.

item.title: An ‘Unprecedented’ Outbreak of Meningitis Raises Alarm in Britain
item.description: Two people have died in an outbreak of meningococcal disease. Many cases were traced to a nightclub in Canterbury that is popular with students at the University of Kent.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Carl Court/Getty Images
item.media:description: Staff and students waiting in line to receive antibiotics at the University of Kent in Canterbury on Monday.

item.title: Judge Strikes Down RFK Jr.’s Vaccine Policies in Blow to Trump’s Health Agenda
item.description: Ruling on a lawsuit brought by several prominent medical organizations, a district court said the federal government had not based its decisions on science in limiting Covid shots and revising the childhood immunization schedule.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Elijah Nouvelage/Getty Images
item.media:description: A meeting of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices in Atlanta in December.

item.title: Administration to Convene ‘God Squad’ With Power to Override Environmental Law
item.description: The meeting, planned for this month, will focus on oil and gas drilling in the Gulf of Mexico.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Interior Secretary Doug Burgum called a meeting of the so-called God Squad. It will be the first gathering of the panel in three decades.

item.title: No H.I.V. Aid Without More Access to Minerals: U.S. Ponders ‘Sticks’ Against Zambia
item.description: A draft State Department memo outlines ways the Trump administration may ratchet up pressure on the African country by ending health support “on a massive scale.”
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen
item.category: President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers unloaded medicines at the Chipulukusu Health Center in northern Zambia earlier this month.

item.title: What Displays Get Scrapped at America’s Parks? It Looks Like Anyone’s Guess.
item.description: President Trump ordered officials to remove information deemed disparaging to the United States. A review of government documents shows little guidance and striking inconsistencies.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: A display on slavery was removed from the Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia in February. A judge has since ordered it restored.

item.title: In Ski Towns, a Bad Snow Year Is Worsening Wildfire Fears
item.description: A record-breaking snow drought has residents worried about much more than slushy slopes.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Nina Riggio
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: A snow-making machine in Nederland, Colo. Some are now aimed at buildings to help protect against the possibility of wildfires.

item.title: Iceland’s Chief ‘Lava Cooler’ Is Bracing for the Next Volcanic Eruption
item.description: Helgi Hjorleifsson, a firefighter, is a leader in a national experiment to steer rivers of lava away from important sites. Some called it crazy, but it worked.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Egill Bjarnason
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times
item.media:description: Helgi Hjorleifsson driving through lava fields in southwestern Iceland in February.

item.title: ‘How Low Can You Go?’ The Shifting Guidelines for Blood Pressure Control
item.description: The number doctors use to demarcate hypertension keeps going down, a trend applauded by many experts, who point to studies linking high blood pressure and dementia.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span
item.media:credit: John P. Dessereau

item.title: How My Trip to Antarctica Unlocked a Family Discovery
item.description: An eight-week voyage to the bottom of the Earth helped the photojournalist Chang W. Lee better understand his late father.
item.dc:creator: Chang W. Lee

item.title: Earlier Cholesterol Testing Can Reduce Heart Attacks and Strokes, New Guideline Says
item.description: Eleven medical organizations advised changes to preventive cardiac care that it says could markedly reduce heart attacks and strokes.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Science Photo Library/Science Source
item.media:description: A finger-prick blood test. The American Heart Association recommends starting to test for levels of LDL cholesterol — the “bad” cholesterol — during childhood, at around age 10.

item.title: E.P.A. Moves to Weaken Limits on Ethylene Oxide
item.description: The gas, ethylene oxide, plays a crucial role in sterilizing medical devices. But long-term exposure is linked to several types of cancer and other ailments.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Gerald Herbert/Associated Press
item.media:description: A chemical and petroleum industrial corridor, a known source of ethylene oxide emissions, in Ascension Parish, La.

item.title: Trump Administration Readies Plans to Dismantle NCAR Research Lab
item.description: Proposals include transferring a supercomputer to the University of Wyoming and shifting a space weather lab to a private company.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Caine Delacy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Center for Atmospheric Research in Boulder, Colo.

item.title: A New Lifeline Helps Inmates Transition to Life Outside the Bars
item.description: Medicaid is now paying for health care in jails and prisons, helping smooth inmates’ return to the community. Corrections and law enforcement officials say they’re all for it.
item.dc:creator: Ted Alcorn and Brian L. Frank
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:credit: Brian L. Frank for The New York Times
item.media:description: Angel Coronado, a community health worker, helps Johnny James Ganados plan for his release from the Elmwood jail in Santa Clara County.

item.title: Palisades Fire Recovery Tests L.A.’s Ability to Invest in Resilience
item.description: Palisades fire victims want to raise money for disaster hardening. Their idea could be a model — if it can get past L.A.’s most vexing housing problems.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Stephen Ross Goldstein for The New York Times
item.media:description: Marcela Silva at the site of her former Pacific Palisades home.

item.title: His Harvard Lab Was Thriving. Then Came the Cuts.
item.description: Will Mair, who studies aging, lost almost all his research funds when the White House cracked down on Harvard. He was wholly unprepared for the upheaval that followed.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Will Mair is a Harvard University professor who studies aging.
